topic.prompt_en: Describe an object that is part of your routine.
topic.learning_goal: Learn to describe an everyday object through function, context, one use case, and personal value.

angles.title: Identify the object clearly
content: State what it is and when it became part of your routine.
If it looks ordinary, explain why it still matters.

angles.title: Describe how you use it
content: Mention when, where, and how often you use it.
Focus on one main purpose instead of listing every possible function.

angles.title: Give one concrete example
content: Use a small real-life moment that shows practical value.
A workday or travel situation usually sounds natural.

angles.title: Explain the value
content: End with why it matters beyond price or appearance.
The value can be convenience, habit change, comfort, or meaning.

sequence.target: Name the object and say how it entered your life.
Explain routine usage and one moment that proves its usefulness.
Summarize the practical or personal value in one clear sentence.

high_score_tips: Choose a real object from your routine so your details sound automatic.
Useful objects score better when you connect them to behavior change, not just function.
A short use case is enough; you do not need a dramatic story.
Keep the tone practical and personal rather than overly emotional.

content.why_it_hurts: The answer starts to sound like a product description instead of personal speech.
content.fix: Pick one or two features that matter in your daily life and explain them properly.

content.why_it_hurts: Appearance alone rarely gives enough material for a full answer.
content.fix: Move quickly to use, routine, and one useful moment.

content.problem: Saying it is important without proof
content.why_it_hurts: The listener hears the claim but not the reason behind it.
content.fix: Add one example that shows how the object saves time, reduces stress, or improves a habit.

content.why_it_hurts: The response sounds memorised if it stays at the level of general ideas and never lands on one lived detail.
content.fix: Add one concrete moment, reaction, or small consequence that actually happened to you.

content.problem: Reflection that only repeats the opening
content.why_it_hurts: The ending feels flat if it only restates the topic instead of adding insight or change.
content.fix: Use the final lines to show what changed, what you realised, or how you would respond now.

language.wrong_example: There is a bottle and I use it every day.
language.better_example: The object I want to describe is a reusable water bottle that I use every day.
language.reason: Direct sentence openings sound stronger and more natural in IELTS speaking.

language.wrong_example: It is very convenient and very convenient for me.
language.better_example: It is lightweight, easy to carry, and genuinely time-saving.
language.reason: Replace repeated convenient with more precise reasons.

language.problem: Overusing very or really with basic adjectives
language.wrong_example: It was very good and really important for me.
language.better_example: It turned out to be more meaningful than I expected.
language.reason: A precise evaluation sounds more natural than stacking intensifiers on basic adjectives.

language.problem: Repeating I think in every sentence
language.wrong_example: I think it is useful, and I think it matters because I think it affects daily life.
language.better_example: What makes it useful to me is the way it affects daily life.
language.reason: Removing repeated I think makes the answer sound more confident and more fluent.

core.example: It is a reusable bottle that I carry almost every day.
It is lightweight enough to fit into any bag.
It is durable, so I do not worry about replacing it often.
It may look simple, but it is extremely practical.
I usually refill it two or three times a day.
It has become part of my routine.

extended.note: Good when the object removes small daily friction.
More natural than saying cheap if the quality is good.
Useful for Band 7 descriptions of everyday items.
Strong phrase when an object changes your behavior.
Good for describing health or productivity routines.
Useful for a modest but valued object.

core.example: It has quietly become part of my routine.
I carry it around whether I am working or commuting.
It really comes in handy on busy workdays.
Keeping it nearby helps me stay on track with healthy habits.
It sounds small, but it makes a noticeable difference.

core.meaning_zh: 很自然地融入我的一天
core.example: It fits naturally into my day because I use it so often.

extended.note: A natural environmental angle if the object replaces disposable items.
extended.example: Using it has helped me cut down on waste.

extended.note: Useful when the object reminds you to do something regularly.
extended.example: It acts as a visual cue for me to drink more water.

extended.note: A very natural spoken way to introduce an ordinary item.
extended.example: It does not look like much, but I use it constantly.

extended.note: Good for value without sounding too promotional.
extended.example: It was not expensive, but it has been worth every penny.

framework.goal: Describe one everyday object through function, use case, and value.

moves: Name the object and say when you got it.
Give one quick reason why it is part of your routine.
Explain how often you use it and in what situations.
Add one specific moment that shows why it matters.
Summarize the value in practical or personal terms.
Keep the ending short and realistic.

delivery_markers: What I like most about it is ...

samples.answer:
The object I want to describe is my reusable water bottle. I bought it about two years ago because I wanted to stop buying drinks every day. Since then, I have taken it with me almost everywhere.

It is quite simple in appearance. It is black, lightweight, and easy to carry, so it fits into my work bag without any trouble. I use it in the office, at the gym, and sometimes even when I am travelling. Usually, I refill it several times a day.

One reason it is useful is that it reminds me to drink water regularly. During busy days, I sometimes forget basic things like taking a break. Seeing the bottle on my desk helps me remember. So even though it is a very ordinary object, it has made one small but healthy change in my life.
highlights: Ends with routine impact rather than feature list

opening: The object I want to describe is ...
body: It is quite simple in appearance.
I use it in the office, at the gym ...
closing: So even though it is ordinary, ...

upgrades.improved: It reminds me to drink water regularly.
upgrades.why: Specific function sounds stronger than general praise.
upgrades.note: Try to show one effect in your routine.

upgrades.improved: I have taken it with me almost everywhere.
upgrades.why: This gives a clearer picture of habit.

upgrades.original: It was very important to me.
upgrades.improved: What made it important was the effect it had on the way I handled similar situations later.
upgrades.why: This explains importance through a concrete effect instead of a vague adjective.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want a simple but natural reflective sentence.

upgrades.original: I learned a lot from it.
upgrades.improved: I came away from it with a clearer idea of what I would do next time.
upgrades.why: This sounds more spoken and more specific than a flat learned a lot.
upgrades.note: Good for endings that focus on takeaway and future behaviour.

samples.answer:
I would like to describe a reusable stainless-steel water bottle that has become part of my everyday routine. I bought it a couple of years ago when I realized I was wasting money on bottled drinks and not drinking enough water.

What I like about it is not the design itself, although it is neat and lightweight, but the fact that it serves a very practical purpose. I take it to work, to the gym, and even on short weekend trips. Because it keeps water cold for quite a long time, I actually use it instead of forgetting it in my bag.

A small but useful example is that I keep it next to my laptop while working. It acts as a visual reminder to pause, stretch, and drink water during long afternoons. That may not sound dramatic, but it has made my routine healthier and more consistent, which is why I value it so much.
highlights: More mature focus on purpose rather than looks

opening: I bought it when I realized ...
body: What I like about it is not ... but ...
A small but useful example is that ...
closing: That is why I value it so much.

upgrades.improved: It serves a very practical purpose.

upgrades.original: It reminds me to drink water.
upgrades.improved: It acts as a visual reminder to pause and drink water.
upgrades.why: The image is clearer and more specific.
upgrades.note: Useful when an object changes behavior.

upgrades.improved: What helped most was that it changed the way I judged the situation in practice.
upgrades.why: This sounds more mature and more natural than the vague phrase helped me a lot.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want to show growth without sounding dramatic.

upgrades.improved: It gave me a more balanced way to look at similar situations afterwards.
upgrades.why: Balanced way to look at is a more idiomatic reflective phrase in spoken English.
upgrades.note: Works well in opinion, process, and experience answers.

samples.answer:
The object I would like to talk about is a reusable water bottle, which sounds rather plain on the surface, but it has had a surprisingly steady influence on my daily routine. I bought it during a period when I was working long hours and relying too heavily on coffee and convenience drinks.

What makes it valuable is not only its function but the habit it has helped me build. It is compact, durable, and easy to carry, so it fits naturally into my day whether I am commuting, working out, or sitting through meetings. More importantly, it has become a visual cue for basic self-care. When deadlines pile up, I tend to ignore small physical needs, and that bottle on my desk quietly interrupts that pattern.

I would not describe it as emotionally meaningful in the traditional sense, but it does represent a more mindful version of my routine. In that way, it is a small object with a disproportionate impact.
highlights: Band 8 style comes from control and reflection
Uses precise phrases like visual cue and disproportionate impact

band_guide.focus: Subtle reflection and more precise value
opening: The object I would like to talk about is ...
It has had a surprisingly steady influence ...
body: What makes it valuable is not only ... but ...
closing: In that way, it is a small object with a disproportionate impact.

upgrades.improved: It has helped me build a more mindful routine.
upgrades.why: This sounds more natural and mature than changed my habit.

upgrades.improved: It is a small object with a disproportionate impact.
upgrades.why: The closing sounds more reflective and memorable.
upgrades.note: Use sparingly as a final line.

upgrades.improved: What stayed with me was not the event itself, but the shift it created in my judgment afterwards.
upgrades.why: This upgrade sounds more nuanced and more reflective than a broad claim like it changed me.
upgrades.note: Strong for higher-band closings that need subtlety rather than drama.

upgrades.improved: Its real value lay in the way it quietly reshaped my response patterns over time.
upgrades.why: Quietly reshaped and over time make the reflection sound more precise and more natural.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want a sophisticated closing without sounding academic.

practice.task: Describe an object you use regularly and explain why it matters in your routine.
checklist: Name the object and how it became part of your life.
Explain routine use and one specific example.
End with practical or personal value.
self_prompts: Where do you usually use it?
What small moment proves its value?
Why do you still keep using it?
